search_terms_used: pacing strategy running performance systematic review meta-analysis
even pacing negative split marathon research evidence
positive split running performance race outcome study
pacing self regulation endurance exercise psychophysiology
GPS pacing running race performance monitoring
coverage_notes: Strong coverage across the pacing strategy domain. Includes two systematic reviews (Sha et al. 2024 on marathon pacing; Ramos-Campo et al. 2025 network meta-analysis comparing strategy types), the landmark taxonomy paper (Abbiss & Laursen 2008), a mini-review on negative split physiology and psychology (Grivas 2025), two large-scale data analyses of recreational marathon pacing behavior (Smyth 2018, 2021), a review of elite runner pacing profiles and tactics (Casado et al. 2021), and a practitioner-focused review bridging science to coaching application (Pryor et al. 2020). The Van Hooren et al. 2020 wearable feedback review covers GPS/tech effects on pacing. One gap: no RCTs specifically on pacing interventions in running (the Ramos-Campo NMA covers mostly cycling); the Fernandes & Maldonado 2024 paper on positive splitting with pacers was considered but excluded in favor of higher-quality sources.

papers.title: Pacing strategies in marathons: A systematic review
papers.notes: Comprehensive systematic review of 39 studies (29 observational, 10 RCTs) examining pacing strategies across half-marathon, marathon, and ultramarathon distances. Found ~77% of marathon finishes display positive pacing, 18% negative, and 13% even. Gender and distance are the most influential factors. Essential reference for understanding the landscape of pacing research by distance.

papers.title: Comparative Effects of Pacing Strategies on Endurance Performance: A Systematic Review and Network Meta-Analysis
papers.notes: Network meta-analysis of 24 studies (302 participants, 765 observations) comparing imposed pacing strategies (fast-start, even, slow-start) against self-selected pacing. Found no significant differences between self-selected and imposed strategies across the network. Key implication: experienced athletes' self-selected pacing may already be near-optimal, and forcing a different strategy does not reliably improve performance.

papers.title: Describing and understanding pacing strategies during athletic competition
papers.notes: Landmark taxonomy paper that defined the six canonical pacing profiles: negative, all-out, positive, even, parabolic (J/U-shaped), and variable. Established that short events (<30s) favor all-out strategies while prolonged events (>2min) benefit from even pacing. Foundational reference for all subsequent pacing research; still the most-cited pacing strategy paper in sport science.

papers.title: The physiology and psychology of negative splits: insights into optimal marathon pacing strategies
papers.notes: Mini-review integrating physiological and psychological mechanisms behind negative-split marathon pacing. Covers glycogen sparing, attenuated cardiovascular drift, improved thermoregulation, and reduced lactate accumulation from conservative early pacing. Also discusses training modalities (progressive long runs, fast-finish workouts) and psychological tools (goal-setting, visualization) to develop negative-split capability. Directly actionable for a running coach AI.

papers.title: How recreational marathon runners hit the wall: A large-scale data analysis of late-race pacing collapse in the marathon
papers.notes: Massive data analysis of 4+ million marathon race records defining 'hitting the wall' via pacing data. Found 28% of men and 17% of women experience sustained late-race pacing collapse. Slowdowns more frequent in the 3 years around a personal best (36% vs 23% in other years), suggesting runners push harder near PBs. Critical evidence for coaching recreational runners on pacing discipline and wall-avoidance strategies.

papers.title: Fast starters and slow finishers: A large-scale data analysis of pacing at the beginning and end of the marathon for recreational runners
papers.notes: Analysis of 1.7 million recreational marathon performances examining pacing at race start and finish. Found fast starts tend to be very fast leading to endurance problems, while fast finishes suggest overly cautious earlier pacing. Women pace more effectively than men but can be too cautious, costing minutes overall. Provides empirical evidence that even or slightly positive splits optimize recreational marathon performance.

papers.title: Pacing profiles and tactical behaviors of elite runners
papers.journal: Journal of Sport and Health Science
papers.notes: Comprehensive review of pacing behaviors across all running distances from 100m to marathon in elite competition. All-out strategy in sprints; U-shaped pacing in middle-distance world records; negative pacing with micro-variations in 5000m/10000m championship races. Distinguishes championship racing (tactical, variable) from time-trial/record attempts (even pacing). Essential reference for understanding distance-specific pacing strategies.

papers.title: Real-time feedback by wearables in running: Current approaches, challenges and suggestions for improvements
papers.notes: Review of how wearable devices (GPS watches, accelerometers) provide real-time feedback on pace, biomechanics, and workload during running. Discusses how feedback modality, frequency, and timing affect pacing behavior and performance. A mix of real-time and delayed feedback is most effective. Directly relevant to coaching AI that integrates GPS/wearable data for pacing guidance and feedback design.
